title: High-mobility inertial domain walls driven by spin-transfer torque in a ferrimagnetic spinel oxide
authors: Mingxing Wu, Shilei Ding, Laura van Schie, Shenghao Cai, Yuhao Qiu, Ao Du, Alexander E. Kossak, Rui Wu, Christian L. Degen, Xuegang Chen, Pietro Gambardella
abstract: Efficient electrical manipulation of domain walls is key to developing magnetic devices with fast switching capabilities and low energy consumption. Here we demonstrate Bloch-type domain wall velocities exceeding 1 km s$^{-1}$ in the single-layer ferrimagnetic spinel oxide NiCo$_2$O$_4$ induced by spin-transfer torque at a current density of $2 \times 10^{11}$ A m$^{-2}$. This exceptional domain wall mobility is attributed to the combination of giant nonadiabatic spin-transfer torque, low magnetization, and high spin polarization. Additionally, we report a pronounced domain wall inertia effect in this ferrimagnet due to the large nonadiabaticity of the torque. The characteristic time for domain wall acceleration and deceleration is $\sim 1$ ns, shorter than that reported for typical ferromagnets. Our findings highlight the potential of spinel oxides as a promising platform for engineering high-performance domain wall devices that take advantage of ultrafast ferrimagnetic dynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates exceptionally fast and efficient domain wall motion in a ferrimagnetic spinel oxide using spin-transfer torque. The researchers observed high mobility and a significant inertial effect, positioning this material as a strong candidate for next-generation ultrafast spintronic devices.
main_problem: The challenge of achieving high-velocity, energy-efficient domain wall motion in magnetic devices for spintronic applications like racetrack memories.
main_result: Demonstrated domain wall velocities exceeding 1 km/s in NiCo2O4 with high mobility and a pronounced inertial effect due to large nonadiabatic spin-transfer torque.
method: Used epitaxial growth of NiCo2O4 thin films, scanning NV magnetometry for domain wall structure, and MOKE microscopy for tracking dynamics under current pulses.
model_or_system: Single-layer ferrimagnetic spinel oxide NiCo2O4 (NCO) thin films grown on MgAl2O4 substrates, utilizing a 1D domain wall model to describe dynamics.
key_observables: Domain wall velocity, domain wall width, chirality, nonadiabatic torque parameter (beta/alpha), and energy consumption per volume.
important_parameters: Current density (2 x 10^11 A/m2), characteristic relaxation time (~1 ns), and spin polarization (-0.73).
main_assumptions: Assumes spin-transfer torque is the sole driving mechanism and that the system operates in a regime without Walker breakdown.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Structural and magnetic characterization (XRD, STEM, SQUID). Fig 2: NV microscopy of domain wall structure. Fig 3: Current-driven domain wall displacement and velocity. Fig 4: Evidence of domain wall inertia. Fig 5: Comparative performance of NCO against other magnetic materials.
paper_structure: The paper begins with material characterization, moves to structural analysis of the domain wall, presents dynamic measurements of velocity and inertia, and concludes with a comparative performance analysis against other magnetic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
